ops.body:
# Serial accept queue (the GCD trap)

The accept loop runs on a **serial** DispatchQueue, never a concurrent one: a blocking `accept(2)` on a concurrent queue makes GCD spawn a fresh worker thread per pending block, exploding the thread pool. The serial queue holds exactly one blocked accept; connections are then handed to a concurrent handler queue.

# Connection discipline

- One request line in, one reply line out per connection; reads framed on \n and capped (maxLineBytes, default 64 KiB); the server appends the framing \n.
- Every accepted connection is verified by PeerTrust BEFORE any frame is processed; a failing peer's connection closes with no reply and the handler never runs.
- Each socket carries a per-connection receive timeout (SO_RCVTIMEO) that bounds socket READS — a silent client cannot wedge a handler thread on the read. It does NOT bound the handler itself: once handler(request) starts there is no timeout, and shutdown's handlerGroup.wait() can block indefinitely behind a stuck handler.

# Bind and shutdown

start(): if the path exists, a live peer answering a connect ping refuses the bind (addressInUse); only a dead socket (connect fails) is unlinked and rebound. The socket is chmod 0600 before accepting.

Shutdown order: stop() signals the accept loop → the accept loop itself closes the listener → acceptQueue.sync returns once the loop exits → in-flight handlers drain (handlerGroup.wait()) → the socket path is unlinked. Safe to call more than once.
